specification.topic-statement: The HTTP multiplexer manages concurrent non-blocking HTTP requests by integrating a multi-handle HTTP client into the select-based event loop, driving requests to completion through repeated polling.

scope.in-scope: Request lifecycle (addition, polling, streaming callbacks, completion detection), file descriptor registration with select, timeout hints, response body accumulation, error categorization, and cleanup on cancellation or destruction.
scope.boundary: How the event loop calls into the multiplexer at each iteration is covered by the REPL event loop specification.
How request bodies and response bodies are formatted for specific LLM providers is handled by the provider implementations and is out of scope here.

field.name: URL
field.note: fully qualified HTTP or HTTPS endpoint

field.name: headers
field.note: HTTP request headers as name: value strings

field.name: body
field.note: request body for POST and similar methods; absent for GET

field.name: streaming callback
field.note: invoked with each chunk of response data as it arrives; null if streaming is not needed

field.name: completion callback
field.note: invoked once when the request finishes, with HTTP status, error information, and the full accumulated response body

field.name: outcome
field.note: one of: success (2xx), client error (4xx), server error (5xx), network error

field.name: HTTP status code
field.note: the HTTP response code; absent on network-level errors

field.name: error message
field.note: human-readable description on failure; null on success

field.name: response body
field.note: the complete accumulated response body; valid for the duration of the completion callback only

behavior.request-addition: A request is submitted by supplying the URL, method, headers, optional body, and callbacks. The multiplexer initializes a new transfer handle, configures it with the supplied options, registers a write callback for incoming data, and adds it to the active transfer pool. An internal response buffer (initially 4,096 bytes, growing by doubling) is allocated for the request. The call returns immediately; the request is processed asynchronously on subsequent event loop iterations.
behavior.fd-set-registration: Before each select call, the event loop queries the multiplexer for the file descriptors currently associated with active transfers and for a timeout hint (in milliseconds). The multiplexer populates the read, write, and exception FD sets by delegating to the underlying multi-handle interface. The event loop incorporates these sets into its select call and uses the minimum of the multiplexer's timeout hint and its own timeout.
behavior.polling: When select returns (either because an FD became ready or because the timeout expired), the event loop calls the multiplexer's perform operation. Perform advances all active transfers — reading socket data, parsing response headers, and invoking data callbacks — and returns a count of still-running transfers.
behavior.streaming-callback:
As response bytes arrive on the network, the write callback fires synchronously within the perform call. For each chunk:
1. If a streaming callback was registered for the request, it is invoked with the chunk bytes. This allows the provider to process partial responses (for example, parsing server-sent events) without waiting for the full body. 2. The bytes are appended to the request's response buffer, extending it by doubling capacity as needed. This produces a complete accumulated body regardless of whether a streaming callback is registered.
The response body is always accumulated in full, even when a streaming callback is provided. The completion callback always receives the complete body.
behavior.completion-detection:
After each perform call, the multiplexer checks the transfer completion queue. For each completed transfer:
1. The outcome is determined: 2xx responses are success; 4xx are client errors; 5xx are server errors; cURL-level failures (network timeouts, DNS errors, connection refused) are network errors. 2. The completion callback is invoked with the outcome, HTTP status code (if available), error message (if applicable), and the accumulated response body. 3. The transfer handle is removed from the pool and freed. 4. The request's internal resources (response buffer, header list) are released.
The completion callback must not assume the response body or error message remain valid after the callback returns.
behavior.cancellation-and-cleanup:
When the multiplexer is destroyed or a cancel operation is invoked, all in-flight transfers are stopped:
1. Each active transfer handle is removed from the multi-handle pool. 2. Transfer-specific resources (response buffer, header list, body copy) are freed. 3. If destroying (not just cancelling), the multi-handle itself is released.
Completion callbacks are not invoked for cancelled transfers.
